narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted a 47.7% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application—it's designed to prioritize students with the strongest signals of bar passage success. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5th or 6th decile and signaled potential struggles in later academics to the model. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA was right around the average of 3.21, which helped push the prediction toward bar exam success and softened the overall risk. Your lower-middle family income bracket also worked in your favor compared to higher averages, as it nudged the model toward seeing you as capable of passing. Meanwhile, your LSAT score of 23 was well below the typical 37, contributing somewhat toward the failure prediction, though not as strongly as your Year 3 decile. Your Year 1 decile of 1st had a smaller but similar concerning influence, while your full-time enrollment plans provided a slight boost since most students follow that path. Overall, these academic rankings stood out as the most influential compared to typical applicants, outweighing your strengths in GPA and background. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a modest income background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted preparation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year grades if possible through additional coursework, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. You might also explore part-time programs or gap-year experiences to build a more competitive profile. Keep pushing; law school doors can reopen with these adjustments, and we're here to support your next steps.
